title: GONNA /gôna/ GOING TO
definition: GONNA /gôna/ \br GOING TO \br Informal reduction used for future intentions or predictions.
example1: I'm gonna leave now. \br She's gonna call you later. \br We're gonna be late.
example3: Not used in formal writing. Often follows 'am/is/are'.

title: WANNA /uôna/ WANT TO
definition: WANNA /uôna/ \br WANT TO \br Reduction of 'want to' expressing desire or intention.
example1: I wanna learn Spanish. \br Do you wanna come with me? \br She doesn't wanna talk.

title: GOTTA /góta/ GOT TO
definition: GOTTA /góta/ \br GOT TO \br Reduction meaning 'have got to' (obligation or necessity).
example1: I gotta go now. \br You gotta see this movie. \br He's gotta finish by Friday.
example3: Often used with 'have' → 'I've gotta' but commonly just 'I gotta'.

title: HAFTA /héfta/ HAVE TO
definition: HAFTA /héfta/ \br HAVE TO \br Reduction of 'have to' expressing obligation.
example1: I hafta work tomorrow. \br She hafta take the test. \br They hafta leave early.

title: USETA /iústa/ USED TO
definition: USETA /iústa/ \br USED TO \br Reduction for past habits or states that are no longer true.
example1: I useta smoke, but I quit. \br She useta live in New York. \br We useta play together as kids.
example3: The 'd' in 'used' becomes a 't' sound before 'to'.

title: SUPPOSETA /supósta/ SUPPOSED TO
definition: SUPPOSETA /supósta/ \br SUPPOSED TO \br Reduction meaning expected or intended to do something.
example1: You're supposeta be here at 9. \br I was supposeta call him. \br They're supposeta arrive soon.
example3: The 'd' in 'supposed' becomes a 't' sound before 'to'.

title: OUGHTA /órta/ OUGHT TO
definition: OUGHTA /órta/ \br OUGHT TO \br Reduction of 'ought to' expressing moral obligation or advice.
example1: You oughta apologize. \br She oughta know better. \br We oughta leave now.
example3: Less common but still used in informal American English.

title: KINDA /káinda/ KIND OF
definition: KINDA /káinda/ \br KIND OF \br Reduction used to mean 'somewhat' or 'sort of'.
example1: It's kinda cold outside. \br I'm kinda tired. \br She kinda likes him.

title: SORTA /sórta/ SORT OF
definition: SORTA /sórta/ \br SORT OF \br Reduction similar to 'kinda', meaning 'a little bit' or 'not exactly'.
example1: He's sorta strange. \br I sorta remember that. \br It's sorta like a bird.

title: LOTTA /lóta/ LOT OF
definition: LOTTA /lóta/ \br LOT OF \br Reduction of 'lot of' meaning a large amount.
example1: I have a lotta work. \br There's a lotta people here. \br He ate a lotta pizza.

title: COULDA /cúda/ COULD HAVE
definition: COULDA /cúda/ \br COULD HAVE \br Reduction of 'could have' for past possibility.
example1: I coulda been a contender. \br She coulda called. \br We coulda won.

title: WOULDA /wúda/ WOULD HAVE
definition: WOULDA /wúda/ \br WOULD HAVE \br Reduction of 'would have' for past hypotheticals.
example1: I woulda helped if I'd known. \br She woulda said yes. \br They woulda come.

title: SHOULDA /xúda/ SHOULD HAVE
definition: SHOULDA /xúda/ \br SHOULD HAVE \br Reduction of 'should have' expressing past regret or missed obligation.
example1: You shoulda told me. \br He shoulda studied. \br We shoulda left earlier.
example3: Often used in advice: 'Shoulda, woulda, coulda'.

title: MUSTA /músta/ MUST HAVE
definition: MUSTA /músta/ \br MUST HAVE \br Reduction of 'must have' for strong deduction about the past.
example1: She musta been tired. \br He musta forgotten. \br They musta left already.

title: MIGHTA /máita/ MIGHT HAVE
definition: MIGHTA /máita/ \br MIGHT HAVE \br Reduction of 'might have' for past possibility.
example1: I mighta seen him. \br She mighta left it at home. \br We mighta made a mistake.

title: LEMME /lémi/ LET ME
definition: LEMME /lémi/ \br LET ME \br Reduction of 'let me' used to make a request or offer.
example1: Lemme see that. \br Lemme help you. \br Lemme think about it.

title: GIVME /gívmi/ GIVE ME
definition: GIVME /gívmi/ \br GIVE ME \br Reduction of 'give me' in informal requests.
example1: Givme a break. \br Givme your phone number. \br Givme five minutes.

title: GIMME /gími/ GIVE ME
definition: GIMME /gími/ \br GIVE ME \br Very common reduction of 'give me', especially in fast speech.
example1: Gimme that! \br Gimme a second. \br Gimme your best shot.
example3: Often used in songs and casual conversation.

title: TELLEM /télem/ TELL THEM
definition: TELLEM /télem/ \br TELL THEM \br Reduction of 'tell them' where 'them' becomes 'em'.
example1: Tellem I said hi. \br I toldem already. \br You should tellem the truth.

title: DONCHA /dônxa/ DON'T YOU
definition: DONCHA /dônxa/ \br DON'T YOU \br Reduction of 'don't you' used in questions and commands.
example1: Doncha like it? \br Doncha worry about me. \br Why doncha come over?

title: WONCHA /uônxa/ WON'T YOU
definition: WONCHA /uônxa/ \br WON'T YOU \br Reduction of 'won't you' used in polite requests or rhetorical questions.
example1: Woncha have a seat? \br Woncha join us? \br Woncha stay for dinner?

title: WHATTA /uóta/ WHAT DO YOU / WHAT ARE YOU
definition: WHATTA /uóta/ \br WHAT DO YOU / WHAT ARE YOU \br Reduction of 'what do you' or 'what are you' in questions.
example1: Whatta you mean? \br Whatta you doing? \br Whatta you think?

title: WHADDAYA /uádaja/ WHAT DO YOU
definition: WHADDAYA /uádaja/ \br WHAT DO YOU \br Another reduction of 'what do you' with a flap T.
example1: Whaddaya say? \br Whaddaya want? \br Whaddaya know?
example3: The 't' becomes a flap /d/, 'do' to /a/, 'you' to /ja/.

title: HOWJA /ráudja/ HOW DID YOU
definition: HOWJA /ráudja/ \br HOW DID YOU \br Reduction of 'how did you' in casual speech.
example1: Howja do that? \br Howja get here? \br Howja know?
example3: 'Did' reduces to /d/ and merges with 'you' to /dja/.

title: DIDJA /dídja/ DID YOU
definition: DIDJA /dídja/ \br DID YOU \br Reduction of 'did you' in yes/no questions.
example1: Didja see that? \br Didja eat yet? \br Didja call him?

title: COULDJA /cúdja/ COULD YOU
definition: COULDJA /cúdja/ \br COULD YOU \br Reduction of 'could you' for polite requests.
example1: Couldja help me? \br Couldja pass the salt? \br Couldja speak louder?

title: BETCHA /béxa/ BET YOU
definition: BETCHA /béxa/ \br BET YOU \br Reduction of 'bet you' meaning 'I'm sure you will/would'.
example1: Betcha forgot your keys. \br Betcha can't do it. \br Betcha he's lying.

title: GOTCHA /góxa/ GOT YOU
definition: GOTCHA /góxa/ \br GOT YOU \br Reduction meaning 'I understand' or 'I caught you'.
example1: Gotcha! That's funny. \br I gotcha now. \br Gotcha, I'll do it.

title: Flap T: WATER /uóra/
definition: WATER /uóra/ \br PRONUNCIATION: flapped T \br In American English, T between vowels becomes a quick flap (like a soft D/R). \br Spoken as /uóra/
example1: Could I have a glass of water? \br The water is cold. \br She waters the plants.
example3: Other examples: better, butter, letter, city.

title: Flap T: LITTLE /líru/
definition: LITTLE /líru/ \br PRONUNCIATION: flapped T + dark L \br The 'tt' is flapped, final 'le' becomes syllabic L. \br Spoken as /líru/
example1: I have a little dog. \br Can you speak a little slower? \br A little bit of sugar.
example3: In fast speech, 'little' sounds like 'liddle' or 'lil'.

title: Flap T: BETTER /béra/
definition: BETTER /béra/ \br PRONUNCIATION: flapped T \br The double T becomes a flap. \br Spoken as /béra/
example1: I feel much better today. \br You better go now. \br Which one is better?

title: Flap T: CITY /síri/
definition: CITY /síri/ \br PRONUNCIATION: flapped T \br The 't' between vowels becomes a flap. \br Spoken as /síri/
example1: I live in a big city. \br The city is noisy. \br Which city do you prefer?

title: Flap T: BUTTER /bóra/
definition: BUTTER /bóra/ \br PRONUNCIATION: flapped T \br The 'tt' becomes a flap. \br Spoken as /bóra/
example1: Pass the butter, please. \br Peanut butter is delicious. \br Butter melts on hot bread.

title: Linking: AN APPLE /a nápul/
definition: LINKING: AN APPLE \br When a word ends with a consonant and the next begins with a vowel, the consonant shifts to the next syllable. \br Spoken as /a nápul/
example1: I'd like an apple. \br He has an idea. \br She took an exam.

title: Linking: AN OLD /a nôld/
definition: LINKING: AN OLD \br The /n/ from 'an' links to 'old'. \br Spoken as /a nôld/
example1: I saw an old car. \br He has an old friend. \br It's an old tradition.

title: Linking: GO OUT /go uáut/
definition: LINKING: GO OUT \br When a word ends in a vowel and the next begins with a vowel, insert a 'w' glide. \br Spoken as /go uáut/
example1: Let's go out. \br I saw a bird. \br She asked.
example3: Insert 'w' after o/u; insert 'y' after e/i.

title: Linking: SEE IT /si iút/
definition: LINKING: SEE IT \br After vowels /i/ or /e/, insert a 'y' glide. \br Spoken as /si iút/
example1: Can you see it? \br I see it. \br We see everything.
example3: The 'y' glide makes the link smooth.

title: Elision: PROBABLY /próbli/
definition: PROBLY /próbli/ \br PROBABLY \br Elision (omission) of the middle syllable 'ba'. \br Spoken as /próbli/
example1: I'll probly be late. \br It's probly going to rain. \br He probly forgot.

title: Elision: COMFORTABLE /cãftérbu/
definition: COMFTERBLE /cãftérbu/ \br COMFORTABLE \br Elision of the 'or' syllable. \br Spoken as /cãftérbu/
example1: This chair is really comfterble. \br Are you comfterble? \br Make yourself comfterble.

title: Elision: EVERY /évri/
definition: EVRY /évri/ \br EVERY \br Elision of the middle vowel, making two syllables instead of three. \br Spoken as /évri/
example1: Evry day I wake up early. \br Evryone is here. \br I know evrything about it.

title: Elision: INTERESTING /íntrestin/
definition: INTERESTIN /íntrestin/ \br INTERESTING \br Elision of the second 'e' and dropping final 'g'. \br Spoken as /íntrestin/
example1: That's an interestin movie. \br It's an interestin idea. \br Very interestin.
example3: Final -ing often becomes -in' in casual speech.

title: Elision: FAMILY /fémli/
definition: FAMLY /fémli/ \br FAMILY \br Elision of the middle vowel, reducing three syllables to two. \br Spoken as /fémli/
example1: My famly is here. \br We're a close famly. \br Famly dinner is at 6.

title: Elision: RESTAURANT /réstrant/
definition: RESTRANT /réstrant/ \br RESTAURANT \br Elision of the middle syllable 'au'. \br Spoken as /réstrant/
example1: Let's go to a restrant. \br That restrant is good. \br I work in a restrant.

title: Elision: DIFFERENT /dífrent/
definition: DIFFRENT /dífrent/ \br DIFFERENT \br Elision of the middle vowel. \br Spoken as /dífrent/
example1: That's a diffrent story. \br It's diffrent now. \br We have diffrent opinions.

title: Assimilation: HAND BAG /rã bág/
definition: HAM BAG /rã bág/ \br HAND BAG \br The final /d/ in 'hand' changes to /m/ because the next sound is /b/. \br Spoken as /rã bág/
example1: She put her handbag on the chair. \br I need a hand brake. \br The band played.

title: Assimilation: GOOD BOY /gúb bói/
definition: GOOB BOY /gúb bói/ \br GOOD BOY \br The final /d/ in 'good' becomes /b/ before /b/. \br Spoken as /gúb bói/
example1: He's a good boy. \br That's a good place. \br I need good paper.

title: Assimilation: WHITE PAPER /uáip pêipor/
definition: WHIPE PAPER /uáip pêipor/ \br WHITE PAPER \br The final /t/ in 'white' changes to /p/ before /p/. \br Spoken as /uáip pêipor/
example1: I need white paper. \br Get white paint. \br A white pillow.
example3: Unvoiced stops assimilate place of articulation.

title: Assimilation: DON'T YOU /dônxa/
definition: DONCHA /dônxa/ \br DON'T YOU \br The /t/ and /j/ coalesce to /tʃ/ (written as /x/).
example1: Doncha like it? \br Doncha worry.

title: Assimilation: COULD YOU /cúdja/
definition: COULDJA /cúdja/ \br COULD YOU \br /d/ + /j/ coalesce to /dʒ/ (written as /dj/).
example1: Couldja help? \br Wouldja come?

title: Assimilation: THIS YEAR /díx íor/
definition: THISH YEAR /díx íor/ \br THIS YEAR \br The 's' in 'this' becomes 'sh' (like the 'x' in 'chuva') before 'y'. \br Spoken as /díx íor/
example1: This year is great. \br This yellow car. \br This young man.

title: Assimilation: HAVEN'T YOU /révenxu/
definition: HAVENCHU /révenxu/ \br HAVEN'T YOU \br The /t/ + /j/ coalesce to /tʃ/ (written as /x/). \br Spoken as /révenxu/
example1: Havenchu seen that movie? \br Havenchu called yet? \br Havenchu finished?

title: Silent letter: KNIFE /náifi/
definition: KNIFE /náifi/ \br SILENT K \br The 'k' is silent in words starting with 'kn'.
example1: He cut the rope with a knife. \br Be careful with that knife. \br I know how to use a knife.
example3: Other words: knee, knock, know, knight, knit.

title: Silent letter: WRITE /ráiti/
definition: WRITE /ráiti/ \br SILENT W \br The 'w' is silent in words starting with 'wr'.
example1: I need to write a letter. \br He writes every day. \br She wrote a book.
example3: Other examples: wrong, wrist, wreck, wrinkle.

title: Silent letter: HONEST /ónest/
definition: HONEST /ónest/ \br SILENT H \br The 'h' is silent in many words of French origin.
example1: He is an honest man. \br To be honest, I don't know. \br She gave an honest answer.
example3: Other silent H words: hour, honor, heir, herb (US).

title: Silent letter: PSYCHOLOGY /saicólodji/
definition: PSYCHOLOGY /saicólodji/ \br SILENT P \br Words starting with 'ps' have a silent 'p'.
example1: I study psychology. \br The psychology of fear. \br He's a psychologist.

title: Silent letter: DEBT /dét/
definition: DEBT /dét/ \br SILENT B \br Words ending in 'bt' often have a silent 'b'.
example1: I have a debt to pay. \br He is in debt. \br The national debt.

title: Silent letter: WRESTLE /résel/
definition: WRESTLE /résel/ \br SILENT T \br In some words, 't' is silent, especially in 'stle' endings.
example1: He loves to wrestle. \br They wrestled for an hour. \br Wrestle with the problem.

title: Silent letter: FASTEN /fássen/
definition: FASTEN /fássen/ \br SILENT T \br The 't' in 'sten' is often silent.
example1: Please fasten your seatbelt. \br Fasten the buttons. \br He fastened the lock.

title: Silent letter: SOFTEN /sófen/
definition: SOFTEN /sófen/ \br SILENT T \br The 't' in 'ften' is silent.
example1: Soften the butter. \br His voice softened. \br The fabric softens with use.
example3: Also 'often' (ófen) – many people pronounce the 't', but silent T is standard.

title: Silent letter: SALMON /sémen/
definition: SALMON /sémen/ \br SILENT L \br The 'l' is silent in some words, especially 'alm' endings.
example1: I love grilled salmon. \br Salmon is a healthy fish. \br Wild salmon is expensive.

title: Silent letter: CLIMB /cláim/
definition: CLIMB /cláim/ \br SILENT B \br Words ending in 'mb' have a silent 'b'.
example1: We climb the mountain. \br He climbed the stairs. \br She has climbed Everest.

title: Elision: CAMERA /cámra/
definition: CAMRA /cámra/ \br CAMERA \br Elision of the middle vowel, reducing three syllables to two.
example1: I need a new camra. \br The camra is broken. \br He took a photo with his camra.

title: Elision: MEMORY /mémri/
definition: MEMRY /mémri/ \br MEMORY \br Elision of the second syllable, reducing three syllables to two.
example1: I have a good memry. \br The memry is stored. \br Childhood memries.

title: Elision: VEGETABLE /véjtabu/
definition: VEGTABLE /véjtabu/ \br VEGETABLE \br Elision of the middle 'e', reducing four syllables to three.
example1: Eat your vegtables. \br The vegtable garden. \br Fresh vegtables are healthy.

title: Elision: TEMPERATURE /témpratura/
definition: TEMPRATURE /témpratura/ \br TEMPERATURE \br Elision of the middle 'e', reducing four syllables to three.
example1: The temprature is high. \br Water temprature. \br What's the temprature today?

title: Coalescence: WHAT YOU /uóxu/
definition: WATCHU /uóxu/ \br WHAT YOU \br The /t/ + /j/ coalesce to /tʃ/ (written as /x/).
example1: Watchu want? \br Watchu doing? \br Watchu mean?

title: Coalescence: NOT YOU /nóxu/
definition: NOTCHU /nóxu/ \br NOT YOU \br Coalescence of /t/ + /j/ → /tʃ/ (written as /x/).
example1: It's not you, it's me. \br That's notchu. \br Notchu again.
